module.title: Module 1: Getting Certified — The Remote Pilot Certificate & UAG Exam
module.description: Orientation to FAA Part 107: what it is, who needs it, how to become a certificated Remote Pilot, what the UAG knowledge exam looks like, how to keep your knowledge current, and how drone registration and Remote ID fit in. A conceptual roadmap for the rest of the course.
learning_objectives: Explain what 14 CFR Part 107 is and distinguish commercial Part 107 operations from recreational flying under 49 U.S.C. 44809.
List the eligibility requirements for a Remote Pilot Certificate and describe the full path to certification, from study through the permanent certificate.
Describe the UAG knowledge exam format - question count, time limit, passing score, and the five ACS knowledge areas with their approximate weights.
Explain recurrent training: the 24-calendar-month knowledge-recency requirement and the free online course that replaced the in-person retest in 2021.
Summarize drone registration, aircraft marking, and the three ways to comply with Remote ID.

lessons.title: Lesson 1: What Part 107 Is and Who Needs It
learning_objectives: Define 14 CFR Part 107 and the small UAS it governs.
Distinguish commercial/non-recreational operations from recreational flying under 49 U.S.C. 44809.
Explain why even some 'free' or non-paid flights still require a Part 107 certificate.

content.html:
The rulebook for commercial drones

14 CFR Part 107 is the section of the federal aviation regulations that governs small unmanned aircraft systems (sUAS) - drones weighing less than 55 pounds at takeoff - when they are flown for any purpose other than pure recreation. A drone operated under Part 107 is still an aircraft in the eyes of the FAA, and the person responsible for it is the Remote Pilot in Command (Remote PIC). To act as a Remote PIC, you must hold a Remote Pilot Certificate with a small UAS rating, which is what this course prepares you to earn.

Part 107 exists because the National Airspace System is shared. The same sky used by airliners, helicopters, and crop dusters is also used by drones, so the FAA sets common rules - altitude limits, visibility minimums, airspace authorizations, and pilot knowledge standards - so that everyone can operate safely. Earning the certificate is the FAA's way of confirming that you understand those rules before you put an aircraft in the air for work.

Commercial vs. recreational: the line that matters

The FAA draws a bright line based on the purpose of the flight, not on whether money changes hands. There are two legal frameworks for civilian drones:

  • Part 107 (commercial / non-recreational): Any flight that is not strictly for personal enjoyment. This includes obvious paid work (surveying, real-estate photography, inspections, mapping) but also flights that further a business or organization even when no one is paid - for example, a volunteer taking aerial photos for a nonprofit, or an employee shooting footage for the company website.
  • Recreational (49 U.S.C. 44809): Flights flown strictly for personal enjoyment, under a community-based organization's safety guidelines, by a pilot who has passed the free TRUST test. The moment a recreational flight has any business, work, or other non-hobby purpose, it is no longer covered by 44809.

If you break any condition of the recreational exception, your flight is judged under Part 107 - and you may face the greater penalties that come with operating commercially without a certificate.

content.text: It is the PURPOSE, not the paycheck. A flight done for free can still require Part 107. If the flight furthers a business, employer, or organization in any way, it is not 'strictly recreational' and you need a Remote Pilot Certificate.

rows: Remote Pilot Certificate (UAG exam + TSA vetting)
One number can cover all your drones

content.text: Part 107 covers small unmanned aircraft that weigh less than 55 lb at takeoff (including payload). Aircraft 55 lb and over fall outside Part 107 and require separate FAA authorization.

content.alt: Decision flowchart: commercial vs recreational drone operation
content.caption: Figure: A decision tree asking 'Is the flight strictly for personal enjoyment?' - Yes leads to the recreational/44809 + TRUST path; No (any business or work purpose) leads to the Part 107 + Remote Pilot Certificate path.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Part 107 (14 CFR Part 107) is the FAA rulebook for small UAS - drones under 55 lb - flown for non-recreational purposes.
The person responsible for a Part 107 flight is the Remote Pilot in Command (Remote PIC), who must hold a Remote Pilot Certificate.
The commercial-vs-recreational line is drawn by the PURPOSE of the flight, not by whether money changes hands.
Recreational flyers operate under 49 U.S.C. 44809 with the free TRUST test; commercial flyers operate under Part 107.
Break a recreational condition and the flight is judged under Part 107.

flashcards.definition: The federal regulation governing small unmanned aircraft systems (drones under 55 lb) operated for non-recreational purposes; it sets pilot certification, operating limits, and airspace rules.
A small unmanned aircraft system weighing less than 55 pounds at takeoff, including everything on board (payload, attachments), together with its control station.
The certificated person who is directly responsible for and the final authority over a Part 107 operation; one Remote PIC is responsible for each flight.
The statutory 'Exception for Limited Recreational Operations'; lets hobbyists fly strictly for personal enjoyment under a community-based organization's safety guidelines after passing TRUST.
The Recreational UAS Safety Test - a free, one-time test (no expiration) required of recreational flyers operating under 44809; it is NOT the same as the Part 107 certificate.

images_needed.caption: Decision flowchart distinguishing recreational (44809/TRUST) from commercial (Part 107) operation based on the purpose of the flight.

lessons.title: Lesson 2: Eligibility and the Path to Certification
learning_objectives: List the eligibility requirements for a Remote Pilot Certificate.
Walk through the step-by-step certification process from IACRA account to permanent certificate.
Describe the role of PSI testing centers, the 8710-13 application, and TSA security vetting.

content.html:
Are you eligible?

Before the FAA will issue a Remote Pilot Certificate, you must meet a short list of eligibility requirements found in 14 CFR 107.61-107.65. To be eligible, a person must:

  • Be at least 16 years old.
  • Be able to read, speak, write, and understand English (this can be waived only for a medical reason such as a hearing or speech condition).
  • Be in a physical and mental condition to safely operate a small UAS.
  • Pass the initial aeronautical knowledge test (the UAG exam) - or, if you already hold a current Part 61 pilot certificate, complete the equivalent free online course instead.
  • Be vetted by the TSA through a security background check, which runs automatically during the application.

Notice what is not on the list: you do not need to already be an airplane or helicopter pilot. No prior pilot certificate is required to take the initial UAG test.

The path to certification, step by step

For someone with no prior pilot certificate, the journey from beginner to certificated Remote Pilot follows a predictable sequence:

  • 1. Study. Learn the five knowledge areas (this course). Aim to score consistently above 85% on practice tests before you schedule the real exam.
  • 2. Get an FTN. Create an account in IACRA (Integrated Airman Certification and Rating Application) to obtain your FAA Tracking Number.
  • 3. Schedule and pass the UAG test at a PSI Services testing center, bringing a government photo ID. You need 70% to pass.
  • 4. Apply. Complete FAA Form 8710-13 (Remote Pilot Certificate application) through IACRA, using your knowledge test report.
  • 5. TSA vetting runs automatically in the background.
  • 6. Temporary certificate. Once vetting clears, you receive a temporary certificate by email - and may begin flying commercially immediately.
  • 7. Permanent certificate arrives later by mail.
  • 8. Register the drone (covered in Lesson 5).

content.text: The temporary certificate is what lets you start working. As soon as TSA vetting clears and your temporary certificate is issued by email, you are legally a Remote PIC - you do not have to wait for the plastic card in the mail.

sections.title: What if I already hold a Part 61 pilot certificate?
sections.content: If you hold a current Part 61 pilot certificate (other than student pilot) and have completed a flight review within the previous 24 months, you may skip the in-person UAG knowledge test. Instead you complete a free online training course on FAASafety.gov, then apply through IACRA the same way. The eligibility, application, and TSA-vetting steps are otherwise the same.

sections.title: What is IACRA, and what is the 8710-13?
sections.content: IACRA is the FAA's online application system for airman certificates. Inside IACRA you fill out FAA Form 8710-13, the application specific to a remote pilot certificate. IACRA also issues your FAA Tracking Number (FTN), which ties your test result to your application.

content.caption: Figure: A horizontal pipeline showing the certification steps - Study, IACRA/FTN, Pass UAG at PSI, File 8710-13, TSA vetting, Temporary certificate (email), Permanent certificate (mail), Register drone.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Eligibility: at least 16, able to read/speak/write/understand English, in safe physical and mental condition, pass the knowledge test, and pass TSA vetting.
No prior pilot certificate is required for the initial UAG test.
The process: study, get an FTN in IACRA, pass the UAG at a PSI center (70%), file Form 8710-13, clear TSA vetting, receive temporary then permanent certificate.
The temporary certificate (emailed after TSA clears) lets you begin flying commercially right away.
Part 61 pilots may substitute a free online course for the knowledge test.

flashcards.definition: Integrated Airman Certification and Rating Application - the FAA's online system where you create an account, get an FAA Tracking Number, and file Form 8710-13.
The application for a Remote Pilot Certificate, completed (usually in IACRA) after passing the UAG knowledge test.
The FAA's testing vendor; you schedule and take the UAG knowledge test at a PSI testing center, bringing a government-issued photo ID.
The automatic background/security check the Transportation Security Administration runs during the certificate application; you cannot be issued a certificate until it clears.
The certificate emailed after TSA vetting clears; it authorizes you to act as Remote PIC immediately, before the permanent card arrives by mail.

images_needed.caption: Step-by-step pipeline of the Part 107 certification process from study through registration of the drone.

lessons.title: Lesson 3: The UAG Knowledge Exam
learning_objectives: State the format of the UAG exam - question count, time limit, and passing score.
Name the five ACS knowledge areas and their approximate weights.
Describe practical exam logistics: PSI centers, retake waiting period, and the modern chart images on the test.

content.html:
What the test looks like

The initial exam is called the Unmanned Aircraft General - Small (UAG) knowledge test. It is built entirely from the Remote Pilot - Small UAS Airman Certification Standards (ACS), the FAA document that lists everything you can be tested on. The format is consistent and worth memorizing:

  • 60 multiple-choice questions, each with three options (A, B, C) and a single correct answer.
  • 2 hours (120 minutes) to complete the test - roughly two minutes per question.
  • 70% to pass - that means about 42 of 60 correct.
  • Taken at a PSI testing center; the fee is set by PSI (commonly around $175, and subject to change).

If you do not pass, you must wait 14 calendar days before retaking it. Your passing knowledge-test report is then valid for a period of time (generally 24 calendar months) for you to apply for the certificate, so apply promptly after you pass.

The five knowledge areas and their weights

The ACS organizes the test into five Areas of Operation. The approximate share each contributes to your 60 questions tells you where to focus your study. Operations is by far the largest area, and together Operations + Airspace make up roughly half to two-thirds of the exam.

content.text: Study strategy: master Operations and Airspace first. Operations alone is the single biggest area, and the two together are about half to two-thirds of the test. The weights are provider-aggregated from the ACS blueprint and can shift, so confirm against the live ACS (currently FAA-S-ACS-10B).

Modern chart images: as of the 2025-2026 testing updates, the UAG exam uses current real-world chart excerpts (Sectionals, approach charts, enroute low charts) that are NOT all in the old legacy test supplement. You must be able to read live-style charts, not just memorize the old figures.

content.alt: Pie or bar chart of the five ACS knowledge-area weights
content.caption: Figure: A chart showing the five ACS Areas of Operation and their approximate exam weights - Operations (35-45%) as the largest slice, then Regulations and Airspace (15-25% each), Weather (11-16%), and Loading & Performance (7-11%).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The UAG exam is 60 multiple-choice questions (3 options each), 120 minutes, 70% to pass (~42 correct).
It is built from the Remote Pilot sUAS ACS and taken at a PSI testing center.
Fail and you must wait 14 calendar days to retake; a passing report is generally valid 24 calendar months to apply.
Five ACS areas: Regulations, Airspace, Weather, Loading & Performance, and Operations - with Operations the largest (35-45%).
Current chart images appear on the exam, so practice reading real sectional excerpts.

flashcards.definition: The Unmanned Aircraft General - Small exam: 60 multiple-choice questions, 120 minutes, 70% to pass, taken at a PSI center to earn the initial Remote Pilot Certificate.
The FAA document (currently FAA-S-ACS-10B) that defines exactly what the UAG test covers; the exam is built from its five Areas of Operation.
Regulations; Airspace & Operating Requirements; Weather; Loading & Performance; Operations - the five knowledge areas of the UAG exam, with Operations the largest.
70%, which is approximately 42 of the 60 questions answered correctly.
If you fail the UAG knowledge test, you must wait 14 calendar days before you can retake it.

images_needed.caption: Chart of the five ACS knowledge-area exam weights, emphasizing Operations as the largest area.

lessons.title: Lesson 4: Staying Current — Recurrent Training
learning_objectives: Explain that the certificate does not expire but knowledge recency must be renewed every 24 calendar months.
Describe how the 2021 rule replaced the in-person retest with a free online recurrent course.
Identify what proof of currency a remote pilot must retain.

content.html:
The certificate doesn't expire - but your knowledge recency does

This is a point that confuses many new pilots. The Remote Pilot Certificate itself does not expire and carries no printed expiration date. What does have to be kept current is your aeronautical knowledge recency. A remote pilot must renew that recency every 24 calendar months in order to keep exercising the privileges of the certificate.

"24 calendar months" means your currency is good through the last day of the 24th month after you complete the training. For example, training completed any day in June would keep you current through the end of June two years later.

The 2021 change: a free online course, not a retest

Before 2021, staying current meant going back to a testing center and paying for an in-person recurrent knowledge test. The 2021 rule change eliminated that. Now you renew your recency by completing a FREE online recurrent training course on FAASafety.gov - there is no in-person retest. The course is training, not an exam to fail, and it now includes a night operations knowledge area added with the 2021 night-flight rule.

The FAA does not mail you a new certificate after you finish recurrent training. Instead, you must retain the course-completion certificate and be able to present it as proof that your knowledge recency is current. Keep it with your other operating documents.

content.text: Initial test vs. recurrent training: the INITIAL UAG exam is an in-person, scored test at a PSI center (70% to pass). RECURRENT training is a free online course you complete every 24 calendar months - you are not 'retested' and there is no in-person component.

No new card is issued for recurrent training. Your one Remote Pilot Certificate stays the same; what you must keep is the recurrent COURSE-COMPLETION certificate as evidence your recency is current. As of this writing the Part 107 recurrent course is hosted on FAASafety.gov - confirm the current course code on the live site.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The Remote Pilot Certificate never expires and has no expiration date printed on it.
Aeronautical knowledge recency must be renewed every 24 calendar months.
Since the 2021 rule change, recency is renewed by a FREE online recurrent course - there is no in-person retest.
The recurrent course includes a night-operations knowledge area.
The FAA issues no new certificate for recurrent training; retain the course-completion certificate as proof of currency.

flashcards.definition: The currency a remote pilot must maintain to keep exercising certificate privileges; it must be renewed every 24 calendar months.
The recurrent-training interval; recency is good through the last day of the 24th month after the training is completed.
Since 2021, a free online FAASafety.gov course (not an in-person retest) that renews knowledge recency; it includes a night-operations topic.
The proof of recurrent-training completion a pilot must retain and be able to present; the FAA does not issue a new pilot certificate after recurrent training.

images_needed.caption: Timeline showing initial certification, then a recurrent online course renewing knowledge recency every 24 calendar months with no in-person retest.

lessons.title: Lesson 5: Registering the Drone, Remote ID, and the 5 Knowledge Areas Ahead
learning_objectives: Describe Part 107 drone registration and the requirement to mark the registration number on the aircraft.
Explain the three ways to comply with Remote ID.
Preview the five ACS knowledge areas the rest of the course will cover.

content.html:
Registering and marking your drone

A Remote Pilot Certificate certifies you; the aircraft must be registered separately. Registration is done through FAADroneZone (the FAA's online portal). The key difference from recreational flying is that Part 107 registration is per-aircraft: every individual drone you fly commercially gets its own unique registration number. (Recreational flyers, by contrast, can register once and apply that single number to every drone they own.)

  • Registration costs about $5 and is valid for 3 years (fee subject to change).
  • You must register any sUAS from 0.55 lb (250 g) up to 55 lb. For recreational use, drones under 0.55 lb need not be registered - but all Part 107 aircraft must be registered regardless of weight.
  • The registration number must be marked on the exterior of the aircraft, legible and readable without tools.
  • Carry proof of registration (digital or paper) during operations.

Remote ID: a digital license plate

Remote ID (RID) is often described as a "digital license plate" for drones - it lets the aircraft broadcast identification and location so authorities can identify drones in the air. Broadcast enforcement began September 16, 2023: if a drone requires FAA registration, it must also broadcast Remote ID (unless it is flown inside a FRIA). There are three ways to comply:

  • 1. Standard Remote ID drone: the broadcast capability is built in. It broadcasts the drone's ID, the drone's location and altitude, the control-station (pilot) location, velocity, a timestamp, and emergency status.
  • 2. Remote ID broadcast module: a retrofit add-on for a drone that lacks built-in RID. It broadcasts the drone's ID, its take-off location (not the moving pilot location), and velocity. When using a module, you must keep the drone within visual line of sight.
  • 3. Fly within a FRIA: an FAA-Recognized Identification Area - a defined location (sponsored by community organizations or schools) where drones without RID equipment may be flown, as long as the drone stays within visual line of sight of the pilot.

rows: Built in: drone ID + drone location/altitude + control-station (pilot) location + velocity + timestamp + emergency status
Retrofit add-on: drone ID + take-off location + velocity; drone must stay within VLOS
No RID equipment required; drone must stay within VLOS of the FAA-Recognized Identification Area

content.text: A broadcast module reports the TAKE-OFF location, while a standard Remote ID drone reports the live CONTROL-STATION (pilot) location. This distinction is a common exam point.

content.alt: Three Remote ID compliance options illustrated
content.caption: Figure: Three side-by-side panels - a Standard Remote ID drone broadcasting pilot + drone location, a drone with a retrofit broadcast module broadcasting take-off location, and a drone operating inside a FRIA boundary with no broadcast equipment.

content.html:
The road ahead: the five knowledge areas

The rest of this course is organized around the five ACS Areas of Operation you will be tested on. Here is the map:

  • Regulations (15-25%): the actual rules of Part 107 - operating limits (400 ft AGL, 87 kt / 100 mph, 3 SM visibility, cloud clearance), accident reporting under 107.9, alcohol and drug rules, operations over people, waivers, and Remote ID.
  • Airspace & Operating Requirements (15-25%): airspace classes A-G, reading sectional charts, controlled-airspace authorizations (LAANC and DroneZone), and special-use airspace, TFRs, and NOTAMs.
  • Weather (11-16%): weather sources, decoding METARs and TAFs, density altitude, stability and fronts, and how wind, clouds, and temperature affect a small drone.
  • Loading & Performance (7-11%): weight and balance, payload effects, density-altitude performance, and LiPo battery care.
  • Operations (35-45%): the largest area - crew resource management, aeronautical decision-making and the five hazardous attitudes, physiology, radio and airport operations, maintenance and preflight, emergencies, and night operations.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Part 107 drones are registered individually (per-aircraft) through FAADroneZone; all Part 107 aircraft must be registered regardless of weight.
The registration number must be marked on the aircraft's exterior, and proof of registration carried during operations.
Remote ID has been enforced since September 16, 2023, with three compliance options: a standard RID drone, a broadcast module, or flying within a FRIA.
A standard RID drone broadcasts the live control-station (pilot) location; a broadcast module broadcasts only the take-off location.
The course is organized around the five ACS areas: Regulations, Airspace, Weather, Loading & Performance, and Operations (the largest).

flashcards.definition: The FAA's online portal for registering drones and requesting airspace authorizations and waivers; Part 107 drones are registered here individually.
Per-aircraft registration: every Part 107 drone gets its own unique number, must be registered regardless of weight, and must have the number marked on its exterior.
A drone's 'digital license plate'; required to broadcast since Sept 16, 2023 for any drone that must be registered, unless flown inside a FRIA.
A retrofit add-on that broadcasts the drone's ID, its take-off location (not the live pilot location), and velocity; the drone must stay within VLOS.
A defined area where drones without Remote ID equipment may be flown, provided the drone remains within visual line of sight of the pilot.

images_needed.caption: Three-panel illustration of the Remote ID compliance options: standard RID drone, broadcast module, and operating within a FRIA.
Roadmap graphic of the five ACS knowledge areas the course covers, with their approximate exam weights.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best describes when a drone flight must be conducted under 14 CFR Part 107 rather than the recreational exception?
options: Only when the pilot is paid money for the flight
Whenever the flight is for any purpose other than strictly personal enjoyment
Only when the drone weighs more than 25 pounds
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Whenever the flight is for any purpose other than strictly personal enjoyment
quiz_questions.explanation: The dividing line is the PURPOSE of the flight, not whether money changes hands. Any non-recreational purpose - including unpaid work that furthers a business or organization - requires Part 107. Weight thresholds are separate from this purpose test.

quiz_questions.question_text: You volunteer to shoot free aerial photos for a local nonprofit's fundraising website. Under which framework must you operate?
options: Recreational under 49 U.S.C. 44809, because you are not paid
Part 107, because the flight furthers an organization and is not strictly recreational
Neither - charitable flights are exempt from FAA rules
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Part 107, because the flight furthers an organization and is not strictly recreational
quiz_questions.explanation: Furthering a nonprofit or business is not 'strictly for personal enjoyment,' so it falls under Part 107 even though no one is paid. The recreational exception requires that the flight be purely a hobby.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the maximum takeoff weight for an aircraft to be considered a 'small unmanned aircraft system' under Part 107?
options: Less than 55 pounds
Less than 25 pounds
Less than 0.55 pounds
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Less than 55 pounds
quiz_questions.explanation: A small UAS weighs less than 55 lb at takeoff, including payload. The 0.55 lb figure is the registration threshold for recreational drones, not the definition of a small UAS.

quiz_questions.question_text: A recreational flyer breaks one of the conditions of 49 U.S.C. 44809 during a flight. How is that flight then evaluated?
options: It is automatically forgiven as a first offense
It is judged under Part 107, with the associated penalties
It is still recreational because the pilot intended it to be
quiz_questions.correct_answer: It is judged under Part 107, with the associated penalties
quiz_questions.explanation: If any condition of the recreational exception is broken, the flight loses 44809 protection and is judged under Part 107, exposing the pilot to greater enforcement.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which credential is associated with RECREATIONAL flying, not Part 107?
options: The Remote Pilot Certificate
The TRUST test
FAA Form 8710-13
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The TRUST test
quiz_questions.explanation: Recreational flyers take the free TRUST test (Recreational UAS Safety Test). The Remote Pilot Certificate and Form 8710-13 belong to the Part 107 path.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the minimum age to be eligible for a Remote Pilot Certificate?
options: 14 years old
16 years old
18 years old
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 16 years old
quiz_questions.explanation: Eligibility under 14 CFR 107.61 requires a person be at least 16 years old, able to read/speak/write/understand English, and in safe physical and mental condition.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement about prior pilot experience is correct for the initial Part 107 certificate?
options: You must already hold a Part 61 pilot certificate to take the UAG test
No prior pilot certificate is required to take the initial UAG knowledge test
You must have at least 40 hours of manned flight time
quiz_questions.correct_answer: No prior pilot certificate is required to take the initial UAG knowledge test
quiz_questions.explanation: No existing pilot certificate is required for the initial UAG test. Holding a current Part 61 certificate is only an alternate path that lets you substitute a free online course for the test.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the certification process, what is the purpose of creating an account in IACRA?
options: To schedule the knowledge test with PSI
To obtain an FAA Tracking Number and later file Form 8710-13
To register the drone and obtain a Remote ID
quiz_questions.correct_answer: To obtain an FAA Tracking Number and later file Form 8710-13
quiz_questions.explanation: IACRA issues your FAA Tracking Number (FTN) and is where you complete the 8710-13 application. Scheduling the test is done with PSI; drone registration is done in FAADroneZone.

quiz_questions.question_text: After you pass the UAG test and complete your application, what allows you to begin flying commercially the soonest?
options: The permanent certificate that arrives by mail
The temporary certificate emailed after TSA vetting clears
A receipt from the PSI testing center
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The temporary certificate emailed after TSA vetting clears
quiz_questions.explanation: Once TSA security vetting clears, you receive a temporary remote pilot certificate by email and may begin operating immediately - you do not have to wait for the permanent card.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which automatic step in the application process is a security background check?
options: TSA security vetting
The flight review
The PSI proctoring check
quiz_questions.correct_answer: TSA security vetting
quiz_questions.explanation: The Transportation Security Administration runs an automatic security/background check during the application. It must clear before a certificate can be issued.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pilot already holds a current Part 61 certificate with a flight review completed 10 months ago. What may they do instead of the UAG test?
options: Skip certification entirely - Part 61 already covers drones
Complete a free online training course instead of the in-person knowledge test
Take a shortened 30-question version of the UAG test
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Complete a free online training course instead of the in-person knowledge test
quiz_questions.explanation: A current Part 61 pilot with a flight review within the previous 24 months may complete a free online course in place of the UAG test, then apply through IACRA as usual.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many questions are on the UAG knowledge test and how long do you have?
options: 60 questions in 120 minutes
100 questions in 180 minutes
40 questions in 90 minutes
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 60 questions in 120 minutes
quiz_questions.explanation: The UAG exam is 60 multiple-choice questions with a 2-hour (120-minute) time limit, about two minutes per question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What score is required to pass the UAG knowledge test?
quiz_questions.explanation: You need 70% to pass, which is approximately 42 of the 60 questions correct.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which ACS Area of Operation makes up the LARGEST share of the UAG exam?
options: Regulations
Weather
Operations
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Operations
quiz_questions.explanation: Operations is the single largest area at roughly 35-45% of the test. It covers CRM, ADM and hazardous attitudes, physiology, maintenance/preflight, emergencies, and night operations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which list correctly names the five ACS Areas of Operation tested on the UAG exam?
options: Regulations; Airspace & Operating Requirements; Weather; Loading & Performance; Operations
Navigation; Communications; Maintenance; Weather; Law
Charts; Batteries; Insurance; Weather; Ethics
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Regulations; Airspace & Operating Requirements; Weather; Loading & Performance; Operations
quiz_questions.explanation: The five ACS areas are Regulations, Airspace & Operating Requirements, Weather, Loading & Performance, and Operations.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you fail the UAG knowledge test, how long must you wait before retaking it?
options: 24 hours
14 calendar days
30 calendar days
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 14 calendar days
quiz_questions.explanation: A failed UAG test requires a 14-calendar-day wait before you may retake it.

quiz_questions.question_text: Where is the UAG knowledge test administered?
options: Online from home with remote proctoring
At a PSI Services testing center
At any FAA Flight Standards District Office
quiz_questions.correct_answer: At a PSI Services testing center
quiz_questions.explanation: PSI Services is the FAA's testing vendor; the in-person UAG test is taken at a PSI center, where you must present a government photo ID.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement about the Remote Pilot Certificate's expiration is correct?
options: The certificate expires every 24 months and must be reissued
The certificate does not expire, but knowledge recency must be renewed every 24 calendar months
The certificate expires every 5 years
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The certificate does not expire, but knowledge recency must be renewed every 24 calendar months
quiz_questions.explanation: The certificate itself has no expiration date. What must be kept current is aeronautical knowledge recency, renewed every 24 calendar months.

quiz_questions.question_text: Since the 2021 rule change, how does a Part 107 pilot renew knowledge recency?
options: By taking a paid in-person recurrent knowledge test
By completing a free online recurrent training course
By re-doing the full 60-question UAG exam
quiz_questions.correct_answer: By completing a free online recurrent training course
quiz_questions.explanation: The 2021 rule replaced the paid in-person recurrent test with a free online recurrent training course - it is training, not a retest, and it includes a night-operations topic.

quiz_questions.question_text: After completing recurrent training, what does the FAA give the pilot as evidence of currency?
options: A brand-new Remote Pilot Certificate by mail
Nothing - the pilot retains the course-completion certificate as proof
A new endorsement printed on the existing plastic card
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Nothing - the pilot retains the course-completion certificate as proof
quiz_questions.explanation: The FAA does not issue a new certificate after recurrent training. The pilot must retain and be able to present the course-completion certificate as proof of currency.

quiz_questions.question_text: A remote pilot completes recurrent training on June 12. Through approximately when is their knowledge recency valid?
options: Through June 12 two years later, to the exact day
Through the last day of June two years later (end of the 24th calendar month)
Through December 31 of the same year
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Through the last day of June two years later (end of the 24th calendar month)
quiz_questions.explanation: '24 calendar months' runs through the last day of the 24th month after completion, so training done in June is good through the end of June two years later - not the exact day.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which knowledge area was added to recurrent training in connection with the 2021 rules?
options: Night operations
Beyond visual line of sight (BVLOS)
International operations
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Night operations
quiz_questions.explanation: The 2021 rule permitted night flight without a waiver, and a night-operations knowledge area was added to the recurrent training course.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does Part 107 drone registration differ from recreational registration?
options: Part 107 drones do not need to be registered at all
Each Part 107 drone is registered individually, rather than one number covering all of a pilot's drones
Part 107 registration never expires
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Each Part 107 drone is registered individually, rather than one number covering all of a pilot's drones
quiz_questions.explanation: Part 107 registration is per-aircraft: every drone gets its own unique number. Recreational flyers can register once and apply that single number to all their drones.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is a requirement for a registered Part 107 drone?
options: The registration number must be marked on the exterior, legible without tools
The registration number may be stored only in an app, never on the aircraft
Registration is required only for drones over 25 pounds
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The registration number must be marked on the exterior, legible without tools
quiz_questions.explanation: The registration number must be marked on the aircraft's exterior and be readable without tools. All Part 107 aircraft must be registered regardless of weight, and proof of registration must be carried during operations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT one of the three ways to comply with Remote ID?
options: Operating a standard Remote ID drone with built-in broadcast
Attaching a Remote ID broadcast module
Filing a Remote ID waiver before every flight
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Filing a Remote ID waiver before every flight
quiz_questions.explanation: The three compliance options are a standard Remote ID drone, a broadcast module, or flying within a FRIA. Filing a per-flight waiver is not one of them.

quiz_questions.question_text: What location does a Remote ID BROADCAST MODULE report, compared with a standard Remote ID drone?
options: The live control-station (pilot) location, same as a standard drone
The drone's take-off location, whereas a standard RID drone reports the live control-station location
It reports no location at all
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The drone's take-off location, whereas a standard RID drone reports the live control-station location
quiz_questions.explanation: A broadcast module reports the take-off location and requires the drone to stay within VLOS, while a standard Remote ID drone broadcasts the live control-station (pilot) location plus drone position, velocity, timestamp, and emergency status.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a FRIA in the context of Remote ID?
options: A federal registration invoice and authorization
An FAA-Recognized Identification Area where drones without Remote ID may fly within VLOS
A required insurance certificate for commercial drones
quiz_questions.correct_answer: An FAA-Recognized Identification Area where drones without Remote ID may fly within VLOS
quiz_questions.explanation: A FRIA is a defined area, sponsored by community organizations or schools, where drones lacking Remote ID equipment may be flown as long as the drone stays within visual line of sight.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which portal is used to register a Part 107 drone?
options: IACRA
FAADroneZone
PSI Services
quiz_questions.correct_answer: FAADroneZone
quiz_questions.explanation: Drones are registered through FAADroneZone. IACRA is for the pilot certificate application; PSI is the testing vendor.
